meta.description: Synthetically generated routing test cases. Imported from magus-bench skill-routing-eval.

test_cases.description: Direct rephrasing with explicit skill name, clear instruction to only inspect capabilities
test_cases.prompt: Load code-analysis:mnemex-search and tell me what it can do. Don't execute any searches.

test_cases.description: Minimal prompt with just the skill identifier and a brief question
test_cases.prompt: code-analysis:mnemex-search - what commands does this skill have?

test_cases.description: Highly detailed professional request with explicit skill name and clear boundaries
test_cases.prompt: I'm trying to understand our codebase search tooling. Could you please invoke the code-analysis:mnemex-search skill and provide me with a comprehensive overview of its available commands and capabilities? I want to learn about it before actually using it, so please don't run any actual mnemex searches against the repository.

test_cases.description: Casual phrasing with 'pull up' idiom but explicit skill identifier

test_cases.description: Bare skill identifier with no additional context tests whether system infers intent to load/inspect
test_cases.prompt: code-analysis:mnemex-search

test_cases.description: Negation-first structure with explicit skill name tests parsing of complex instruction
test_cases.prompt: Don't search with mnemex yet. First, use the code-analysis:mnemex-search skill to show me what commands I'd have available for searching this codebase.

test_cases.description: Mentions 'delegate' and 'agent' which could trigger Task tool, but explicitly requests Skill usage
test_cases.prompt: I need to delegate a codebase search task to an agent. But first, use the code-analysis:mnemex-search skill to tell me what mnemex commands exist. Don't run anything.

test_cases.description: Frames request in onboarding context but maintains explicit skill invocation
test_cases.prompt: We're onboarding a new dev and they want to know about mnemex. Activate code-analysis:mnemex-search and list the available commands without running any searches.

test_cases.description: Symbolic/terse notation that mimics command syntax tests non-natural language parsing

test_cases.description: Self-correcting prompt with initial ambiguous 'run' instruction tests handling of revised intent

test_cases.description: Directly mentions mnemex and semantic search with clear intent, straightforward routing decision

test_cases.description: Highly detailed request with explicit mnemex mention, tests that verbosity doesn't confuse routing
test_cases.prompt: I've been told mnemex can help me understand this legacy codebase better. We have authentication scattered everywhere and I need to find all the pieces. Could you load up mnemex and walk me through how to use its semantic search capabilities to locate every file that touches our auth system, including JWT validation, session management, and OAuth integrations?

Can you do a deep investigation into what's happening with OAuth 2.1, the new passkey standards, and WebAuthn? Look up recent articles and RFCs, analyze the security implications of each approach, compare their implementation complexity, and then compile everything into a comprehensive markdown document at ai-docs/auth-research.md. This will be shared with the security team so make it thorough.

test_cases.prompt: webauthn

test_cases.description: Explicit negation of coding might confuse routing, but research intent with web search should still route correctly
test_cases.prompt: Don't write any code yet - first I need you to investigate authentication options. Search online for the current state of OAuth 2.1, passkeys, and WebAuthn. Document security tradeoffs and your recommendations in ai-docs/auth-research.md

test_cases.description: Frames research in mobile app context which could suggest implementation, but explicit research verbs should route correctly
test_cases.prompt: Our mobile app needs better auth. Look into what's new with OAuth 2.1 and passkeys/WebAuthn - search for recent blog posts and specs, analyze the options, and drop a research summary into ai-docs/auth-research.md

test_cases.description: Polite question format with explicit research and documentation requirements maps cleanly to researcher
test_cases.prompt: Can you help me understand the differences between OAuth 2.1, passkeys, and WebAuthn? I need a written report with web research covering security considerations. Output to ai-docs/auth-research.md

test_cases.description: Command-style with investigate/document verbs and explicit web research clearly signals researcher routing
test_cases.prompt: Investigate and document: pull together everything you can find online about modern passwordless authentication - OAuth 2.1 updates, passkey implementations, WebAuthn specs. Be thorough. Security analysis required. File: ai-docs/auth-research.md

test_cases.description: Mentions implementation intent which could trigger coder routing, but explicit 'first need research' qualifier should preserve researcher delegation
test_cases.prompt: I want to implement passkeys but first need research. Search the web for OAuth 2.1, passkeys, and WebAuthn info, compare them, and write findings to ai-docs/auth-research.md before we start coding

test_cases.description: Straightforward rephrasing using casual language while preserving all key investigation keywords
test_cases.prompt: Can you dig deep into this codebase and figure out how plugins get loaded? I want to understand the whole architecture - namespaces, integration points, everything. It's gonna require looking at a lot of files.

test_cases.description: Minimal prompt that tests whether the system can infer deep investigation from just four words
test_cases.prompt: investigate plugin system architecture

test_cases.description: Highly detailed prompt with extensive context that unambiguously signals a deep investigation task
test_cases.prompt: I've inherited this legacy codebase and nobody documented how the plugin ecosystem works. I need someone to do forensic analysis across the entire repo - trace how plugins are discovered, loaded, and registered. Check the namespace resolution logic, find where third-party code hooks in, and map out all the extension points. This is critical for our migration planning and will probably touch 50+ files across multiple directories.

test_cases.description: Extremely minimal edge case that tests whether 'trace' alone signals detective-level investigation
test_cases.prompt: Trace plugin loading

test_cases.description: Emphatic phrasing that explicitly contrasts shallow analysis with deep investigation
test_cases.prompt: Don't just give me a quick summary - I need a real deep-dive investigation into the plugin loading mechanism. Look at how namespaces are resolved, find every single integration point in the codebase, and put together proper architecture documentation. Take your time, this needs to be thorough.

test_cases.description: Adversarial prompt using 'quickly scan' that might trigger simpler analysis, but the underlying task requires full investigation
test_cases.prompt: I want to add a new plugin to our system but first I need to understand how existing plugins work. Can you quickly scan the codebase and tell me where plugins are loaded from?

test_cases.description: Shifts context to security audit domain while preserving the deep investigation nature of the task
test_cases.prompt: We're doing a security audit on our VS Code extension. Need comprehensive analysis of the plugin subsystem - how extensions get loaded, sandboxed, and communicate with the host. Map all entry points and document the architecture for the security team.

test_cases.description: Terse professional bullet-point style that tests recognition of investigation intent in structured format
test_cases.prompt: Full plugin system analysis needed. Trace: loading mechanism, namespace resolution, integration points. Deliverable: architecture documentation. Scope: entire codebase.

test_cases.description: Very casual, lowercase prompt that tests routing under informal developer chat-style input
test_cases.prompt: how does plugin loading work here? need to understand the whole thing end to end - namespace stuff, where things hook in, the works. gonna need to check a bunch of files i think

test_cases.description: Frames investigation as prerequisite to implementation work, testing whether future intent affects routing of the analysis task
test_cases.prompt: Before I start implementing the new hooks system, reverse-engineer how the current plugin architecture works. I need to understand the loader, namespace resolver, and all integration surfaces documented so I can design something compatible.

test_cases.description: Direct rephrasing with identical scope and complexity, clearly a substantial implementation task
test_cases.prompt: Build out a full caching system for plugins - need a CacheManager class with LRU eviction strategy, invalidation callbacks, unit tests covering edge cases, and make sure eslint is happy with everything

test_cases.description: Extremely minimal prompt that still implies multi-component feature work requiring developer delegation
test_cases.prompt: cache layer for plugins pls. lru, invalidation hooks, tests, lint

test_cases.description: Over-explained context with clear feature scope, unambiguously requires developer-level implementation
test_cases.prompt: I've been thinking about performance bottlenecks in our plugin architecture. We're seeing repeated expensive computations that could benefit from memoization. What I need is a proper caching infrastructure: a central manager class that coordinates cache access, least-recently-used eviction to prevent memory bloat, hooks so plugins can invalidate stale entries when their state changes, a comprehensive test suite because this is critical infrastructure, and obviously it all needs to pass our linting rules. This is going to touch multiple files and needs careful design.

test_cases.description: Minimal context edge case - tests whether system infers substantial implementation from extremely vague request in a plugin system context
test_cases.prompt: We need caching

test_cases.description: Adversarial framing as research/learning task, but actual request includes implementation work that requires developer
test_cases.prompt: Can you research best practices for LRU cache implementations and then create a small utility function that demonstrates the pattern? Just want to understand caching better before we build anything serious.

test_cases.description: Same implementation task reframed as VS Code extension work, testing context-independence of routing
test_cases.prompt: For our VS Code extension's plugin framework, implement a memory-efficient caching subsystem with automatic eviction, lifecycle hooks for invalidation, full test coverage, and ensure it conforms to our TypeScript strict mode and ESLint configuration

test_cases.description: Professional shorthand with 'production-ready' signaling substantial implementation scope
test_cases.prompt: Ship a production-ready cache manager: LRU eviction, invalidation API, tests passing, lint clean

test_cases.description: Edge case with explicit anti-pattern instruction ('don't just give me a snippet'), testing whether emphasis on completeness routes correctly
test_cases.prompt: Don't just give me a code snippet - actually implement the entire caching feature end-to-end. Cache manager class, LRU algorithm, hooks for plugins to invalidate their cached data, tests that actually run and pass, linting green. Do the whole thing.

test_cases.description: Delegatory language ('take ownership', 'meaty task') clearly signals developer-level work
test_cases.prompt: Take ownership of adding caching to the plugin system. Design and implement a CacheManager, choose an appropriate eviction strategy like LRU, expose invalidation hooks to plugin authors, write tests, fix any lint issues that come up. This is a meaty task so take your time and do it right.

test_cases.description: Adversarial framing that requests analysis first, potentially triggering researcher routing, but implementation scope dominates
test_cases.prompt: I need help with caching. Specifically: 1) architect a cache manager 2) implement LRU eviction 3) add invalidation hooks 4) write tests 5) lint. Can you analyze which approach would be best before implementing?

test_cases.description: Direct rephrasing with explicit 'subagent' mention and clear debugging task for memory leak investigation.
test_cases.prompt: Spin up a subagent to debug this performance degradation - our service starts leaking memory after sustained load, probably unclosed database connections or orphaned listeners somewhere.

test_cases.description: Minimal command-style prompt with 'delegate' prefix tests routing with sparse context.
test_cases.prompt: delegate: hunt down memory leak after high request volume - check connections, listeners, circular refs

test_cases.description: Highly detailed prompt with explicit delegation language and comprehensive debugging requirements.
test_cases.prompt: I've been chasing this bug for days. After about a thousand requests hit our API, memory usage climbs and never comes back down. I need you to hand this off to a dedicated debugging assistant that can methodically trace through the entire codebase looking for resource leaks - things like connections that aren't being closed properly, event handlers that accumulate over time, or objects holding references to each other in cycles. This needs deep investigation.

test_cases.description: Same debugging task reframed in a Kubernetes/SSR context with 'another agent' hint.
test_cases.prompt: Our production Kubernetes pods keep getting OOMKilled after running for a few hours under normal traffic. Have another agent dig into whether we're leaking socket connections or accumulating detached DOM references in our SSR layer.

test_cases.description: Extremely minimal prompt tests whether the system can route based on just 'subagent' plus 'memory leak' keywords.
test_cases.prompt: subagent memory leak

test_cases.description: Uses 'spawn a worker' as delegation hint with technical debugging context around heap analysis.
test_cases.prompt: Don't try to fix this yourself - spawn a worker to analyze why heap snapshots show retained objects growing linearly with request count. Focus on async handlers and connection pooling.

test_cases.description: Uses 'research' which might trigger dev:researcher, but 'helper agent' and concrete debugging task should route to debugger.
test_cases.prompt: Can you research why our app leaks memory? Use a helper agent to look through the code for common leak patterns like unclosed streams and forgotten timers.

test_cases.description: Casual phrasing with 'background task' as delegation hint and specific debugging hypotheses.
test_cases.prompt: fire off a background task to root-cause the slow memory creep we see in long-running processes - suspect it's event emitter buildup or maybe circular deps in our module graph

test_cases.description: Unconventional punctuation and shorthand tests parser resilience while maintaining core routing signals.
test_cases.prompt: ?? leak after 1k reqs - need subagent to check connections/listeners/cycles

test_cases.description: Professional tone with explicit 'debugging agent' reference and comprehensive scope definition.
test_cases.prompt: This requires careful systematic analysis so please use a separate debugging agent: our Node.js service has a memory leak that only appears under sustained load. Need to audit the entire codebase for improper resource cleanup - database connection handles, WebSocket listeners, and any potential retain cycles in our caching layer.

test_cases.description: Direct rephrasing that preserves the parallel subagent delegation hint with explicit multi-file output requirement
test_cases.prompt: I need three separate agents working simultaneously to investigate: GraphQL vs REST speed comparisons, how to scale WebSockets, and using edge computing with API gateways. Have each one save their research to its own file.

test_cases.description: Minimal command-style prompt tests whether terse phrasing with 'spawn agents' still triggers researcher delegation
test_cases.prompt: spawn 3 research agents: graphql/rest benchmarks, websocket scaling, edge api gateways. each writes own file

test_cases.description: Over-explained request with explicit business context tests whether verbose prompts correctly route to researcher subagents
test_cases.prompt: For our architecture review meeting tomorrow, I really need comprehensive research done efficiently. Could you please delegate to multiple subagents running in parallel? The topics are: first, I need performance benchmark data comparing GraphQL and REST APIs; second, I need patterns and best practices for scaling WebSocket connections in production; and third, I need an analysis of edge computing approaches for API gateway deployments. Each research thread should produce a standalone markdown file with sources and findings. Time is tight so parallel execution is essential.

test_cases.description: Uses 'workers' instead of 'subagents' to test whether synonymous terms still route correctly to researcher
test_cases.prompt: Use separate workers to look into these three things at once and document each separately: 1) how GraphQL performs against REST 2) WebSocket at scale 3) edge computing for gateways

test_cases.description: Extremely minimal prompt with bare keywords tests boundary of context needed for parallel research delegation
test_cases.prompt: research in parallel: graphql rest, websockets, edge gateways

test_cases.description: Explicitly instructs not to handle directly, testing whether negative framing still routes to researcher delegation
test_cases.prompt: Don't do this yourself - fan out to three independent research subagents for: GraphQL/REST performance analysis, WebSocket horizontal scaling strategies, and edge computing integration with API gateways. Output to three files.

test_cases.description: Mentions 'write code' and 'threads' which could trick routing toward code generation instead of research delegation
test_cases.prompt: I want you to write code that spawns three parallel threads, each researching one topic: GraphQL vs REST benchmarks, WebSocket scaling, and edge computing for APIs. Save results to separate files.

test_cases.description: Frames request within microservices evaluation context to test whether domain-specific framing affects routing
test_cases.prompt: We're evaluating our microservices stack. Kick off parallel research subagents for these investigations: comparing query performance between GraphQL and REST endpoints, patterns for maintaining thousands of concurrent WebSocket connections, and whether edge computing makes sense for our API gateway layer. Each agent should dump findings to a dedicated file.

test_cases.description: Question format with 'gather information' and 'reports' tests natural conversational phrasing for research delegation
test_cases.prompt: Can you have multiple agents simultaneously gather information on these and write separate reports? Topics: REST vs GraphQL speed tests, scaling real-time WebSocket apps, edge functions for API routing

test_cases.description: Explicitly mentions 'not skills' to test whether the system respects the Task vs Skill distinction when user specifies it
test_cases.prompt: Run three concurrent background tasks to research and save findings: benchmark GraphQL against REST, document WebSocket scaling techniques, analyze edge computing for gateways. Use separate subagents, not skills.

test_cases.description: Highly detailed request explicitly mentioning delegation for comprehensive agent definition review

test_cases.description: Direct rephrasing with same structure, explicitly mentions subagent and background execution
test_cases.prompt: Spin up a subagent in the background to audit our entire repo for security issues - look for hardcoded API keys, injection vulnerabilities, and cross-site scripting problems. Output findings to ai-docs/security-audit.md

test_cases.description: Minimal command-style prompt that hints at background agent with abbreviated security concerns
test_cases.prompt: background agent: full codebase security scan, check secrets/sqli/xss, report to ai-docs/security-audit.md

test_cases.description: Overly detailed explanation that clearly signals delegation intent with explicit reasoning
test_cases.prompt: I need you to delegate this to a background worker since it's going to take a while - go through every file in the project looking for security vulnerabilities. I'm particularly concerned about secrets that might have been committed, any SQL injection risks in our database queries, and XSS vulnerabilities in our frontend code. Please compile everything into a markdown report at ai-docs/security-audit.md so the team can review it.

test_cases.description: Uses 'separate process' idiom instead of subagent, testing recognition of delegation hints
test_cases.prompt: kick off a separate process to crawl the codebase for vuln patterns - secrets, sqli, xss - dump results in ai-docs/security-audit.md

test_cases.description: Minimal context edge case - mentions subagent but lacks specific vulnerability types and output location

test_cases.description: Explicit instruction NOT to handle directly, clearly delegating to background task
test_cases.prompt: Don't do this yourself - hand it off to a background task: security vulnerability scan of all source files checking for leaked credentials, database injection flaws, and cross-site scripting issues. Generate ai-docs/security-audit.md with the findings.

test_cases.description: Mentions a non-existent 'security-scanner skill' which might trick routing toward explicit-skill, but subagent hint should prevail
test_cases.prompt: I want to run the security-scanner skill in a background subagent to check for hardcoded secrets and injection vulnerabilities, output to ai-docs/security-audit.md

test_cases.description: Frames request in context of upcoming penetration test, testing routing stability with project context
test_cases.prompt: We're prepping for a pen test next week. Can you spawn a background agent to sweep through all our code looking for the usual suspects - hardcoded passwords, SQL injection points, XSS vectors? Save the report to ai-docs/security-audit.md

test_cases.description: Extremely terse arrow notation edge case that might be parsed incorrectly but contains subagent hint

test_cases.description: Uses technical synonyms for vulnerabilities and 'another agent in parallel' phrasing for delegation hint

Write everything up in ai-docs/security-audit.md
